toast.cmsUnavailable: Could not reach Copernicus Marine. Showing cached data only — try again in a moment.
toast.overlaysUnavailable: Could not reach EMODnet / EEA overlay services. Sectoral layers may be incomplete — try again in a few seconds.

timeSlider.scrubAria: Drag to change the anomaly date

interop.aria: Open standards served by this dashboard

coach.step1Body: The Mediterranean basin shows live sea-surface-temperature anomalies — red is warmer than normal for this day of the year, blue is cooler. Drag, zoom, and use the time slider to scrub through history.
coach.step2Title: Click any bubble to learn about an event
coach.step2Body: Each colored bubble is a detected marine heatwave. Bigger and darker means more severe. Click one to see its dates, peak temperature, and sectoral impact (aquaculture, MPAs, seagrass).
coach.step3Body: The keyboard help button (bottom-right of the map) lists every shortcut — including 1-4 to toggle layers, Space to play the time animation, and B to draw a bbox filter.

eventList.aria: Marine heatwave events, sorted by peak intensity
eventList.itemAria: {category} marine heatwave, {start} to {end}, peak +{peak} degrees Celsius above mean, click to focus
eventList.selectedAnnounce: Selected: {category} marine heatwave, {start} to {end}, peak +{peak} degrees Celsius

about.title: About MHEAT
about.tagline: An open dashboard for Mediterranean marine heatwaves.
about.whatBody: MHEAT detects, classifies and visualises marine heatwaves in the Mediterranean Sea. It applies the Hobday et al. 2016 algorithm to live Copernicus Marine sea-surface-temperature observations, then joins each event with sectoral overlays — aquaculture sites, Natura 2000 marine protected areas, and seagrass meadows — so anyone can see where the warming was, how severe it was, and what was inside it.
about.whoBody: Marine biologists and oceanographers who need scientifically defensible event detection. Fisheries and aquaculture managers tracking damage to commercial farms. Marine Protected Area wardens and EU policy analysts measuring exposure of protected ecosystems. Climate scientists studying multi-decadal trends. Journalists and educators looking for clear, sourced numbers. NGOs and the curious public who want to understand the state of the sea.
about.dataTitle: Where does the data come from?
about.dataBody: Sea-surface temperature: Copernicus Marine Med-MFC reanalysis 1993-2019 plus the live near-real-time analysis. Aquaculture sites: EMODnet Human Activities (finfish farms). Marine Protected Areas: European Environment Agency Natura 2000 (Habitats + Birds Directives). Seagrass meadows: EMODnet Seabed Habitats (Posidonia + Cymodocea). Detection method: Hobday et al. 2016 — Frontiers in Marine Science. All sources are open and credited on every page.
about.grantBody: MHEAT is built under the EDITO FSTP (Financial Support to Third Parties) Call #1 of the European Digital Twin of the Ocean. It is designed to plug directly into the EDITO Datalab via STAC, OGC API, and ARCO Zarr, so users can move from this dashboard to a JupyterLab notebook in one click.
about.techBody: TRL 7 — system prototype demonstrated in operational environment. Backend: Python + FastAPI + xarray + Zarr. Frontend: React + TypeScript + MapLibre. Standards surfaces: OGC API – Features 1.0, STAC 1.0, ARCO Zarr, GeoParquet, Prometheus metrics. Reproducibility: SHA-256 manifest of every derived artifact.
about.licenseBody: Code: MIT license. Derived data: CC-BY 4.0. Source data: subject to the upstream licenses of Copernicus Marine, EMODnet and EEA Natura 2000 (all open).
